Programming

Artificial Intelligence

Machine Learning

16 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

How We Got Here — Episode 5 of ~26. We are walking the AI timeline one contribution at a time.

Picture the last time you needed a quick classifier.
You had a few thousand labeled emails, each tagged spam or not-spam, and you needed something that could look at the next one and answer a single yes-or-no question. You were not, for a two-hour task on a Tuesday, going to spin up a giant neural network and babysit it through training.
So you did the sensible thing. You imported scikit-learn, reached for a classifier (an SVM, maybe, or logistic regression), called .fit(), and twenty seconds later you had a working spam filter. No GPU. No hyperparameter séance. It just worked.
That instinct (“just use an SVM”) is so baked into how we work that it barely registers as a choice. Most of us learned it as a rule of thumb and never asked where it came from.
Here’s where it came from. And here’s the part that should stop you.
For about ten years, that instinct wasn’t just the convenient choice. It was the frontier. The SVM (the support vector machine, the thing you now reach for without thinking) was the most advanced idea in the field during the 1990s, the one the smartest people in machine learning were most excited about. The neural network, the model ancestor you probably interact with today, was the embarrassing cousin. The dead end. The approach you’d apologize for bringing up at a conference.
This is the episode where the wheel turns the wrong way. Where the brain-inspired stuff loses badly and mostly deserves to. For a while.

Quick recap
If you’re just joining, here’s where we are.
Last week, in Episode 4, we followed a neural network to its first real job. In 1989, Yann LeCun and his colleagues at Bell Labs built a convolutional network (a net whose neurons are small detectors slid across an image) and taught it to read handwritten ZIP codes off actual mail from Buffalo. It worked. It went into production. By the early 2000s, descendants of that system were reading something like a tenth of all the paper checks in the United States.
And the field mostly walked away from it. The energy, the funding, the prestige. Most of it went somewhere else entirely, for more than a decade.
I left you with a question at the end of that piece. If convolutional nets were reading checks in the corner, what was everyone else so excited about? What shiny thing made a working, deployed, money-moving neural network look like a narrow trick not worth chasing?
This episode is the answer. And to understand why it won, you have to see how good it really was.

The best line, not just a line
Let’s go all the way back to Episode 1 for a second, because the SVM is easiest to understand as an upgrade to something you already met.
The perceptron, back in 1958, did one thing. You gave it examples from two categories (say, dots on a page that were either red or blue) and it found a line that separated them. Red on one side, blue on the other. That’s binary classification, the same yes-or-no job your spam filter does, and it’s the oldest job in the whole field.
But the perceptron had a quiet flaw. It found a line. Any line that separated the training examples would satisfy it, and it stopped the moment it found one. If the red and blue dots left a wide gap between them, the perceptron didn’t care; it might jam its line right up against the blue dots, as long as no example landed on the wrong side. It solved the problem. It just didn’t solve it well.
Now here’s the question the support vector machine asks, and once you hear it you can’t unhear it: of all the lines that separate the two groups (and there are infinitely many), which one is best?
The answer is elegant and obviously correct when you sit with it. The best boundary leaves the widest possible gap on either side. Imagine you’re not drawing a line at all, but paving the widest street you can between the two groups without any dot falling in the road. The best separator runs right down the center of that street. The street has a name: the margin. The whole method is built around making it as wide as possible.
Why does that matter? Because the width of the street is a bet about the future. The training dots are the examples you’ve already seen; the real test is the new email that lands near the boundary. If your separator hugs one group tightly, a slightly unusual new example can fall on the wrong side. But if there’s a wide, empty street between the groups, new examples have room to be a little unusual and still land where they belong. A wide margin is a machine that’s harder to fool.
And here’s the detail that gives the method its name: to define that widest street, you don’t need all your data. Just the handful of dots sitting right on its edge, the ones that determine how wide it can get before it touches something. Those borderline examples are the support vectors. They hold the boundary in place the way a few tent poles hold up the whole tent. Every other dot, comfortably far from the border, could vanish and the boundary wouldn’t move.
So that’s the first idea: the perceptron finds a line, and the SVM finds the best line, defined by the few examples that sit closest to the fight. A nice refinement. But not yet the thing that made the field fall in love. That’s next.

The trick that made it elegant
There’s an obvious problem with everything I just said. I’ve been talking about drawing a straight line between two groups. But most real data can’t be split by a straight line.
Think about it. Spam isn’t neatly on one side of some clean divide. Picture red dots forming a ring, with blue dots clustered in the middle of the ring. There is no straight line in the world that puts all the red on one side and all the blue on the other. The perceptron would fail here. A basic SVM, drawing a straight boundary, would fail here too.
The fix sounds like cheating, and the fact that it isn’t cheating is the beautiful part.
The idea is this: data that can’t be separated by a straight line in its current form can often become separable if you lift it into a higher-dimensional space. Take those red dots in a ring and the blue dots in the middle, and give each dot a new coordinate: a height based on how far it sits from the center. The blue dots near the center barely rise; the red dots in the ring get lifted way up. The flat, tangled picture becomes a three-dimensional one, red dots raised on the outside, blue dots low in the middle. And now you can slide a flat sheet between them. A boundary that was impossible in two dimensions becomes easy in three. Add dimensions until the tangle pulls apart, then separate it with the clean maximum-margin boundary you already know how to find.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

But there’s a catch, and it’s where things get hard. For complicated data you might need to lift into a space with thousands of dimensions, or millions, or infinitely many (for some of the most useful cases). You cannot compute infinitely many coordinates for every point. The whole approach should collapse under its own weight the moment the data gets interesting.
Here is the move that saves it, and it deserves its reputation.
It turns out that to find the maximum-margin boundary, the math never actually needs the coordinates of your points in that giant new space. It only ever needs one thing: for any two points, a single number saying how similar they are once lifted. For the right choice of lift, there’s a shortcut. A formula that takes two points in the original low-dimensional space and hands you back that similarity number directly, as if you had lifted both points into the enormous space and compared them there. Without ever going.
That shortcut is called a kernel, and the sleight of hand is the kernel trick. You get all the power of separating your data in a million-dimensional space while doing your actual arithmetic in the small, cheap, original one. You judge how alike two things are in the big space without ever building the big space.
This is the part I keep coming back to. Not the result. The audacity of the shortcut. A researcher in the 1990s could take hopelessly tangled data, conceptually lift it into a space too large to even represent in a computer, find the best boundary there, and run the whole calculation on a modest workstation, because the kernel let them skip the trip entirely. The first time that clicked for me, I laughed out loud at my desk. It felt less like engineering and more like getting away with something. And that elegance is the reason for everything that happened next.

Why the field fell in love
Now put yourself in the shoes of a machine learning researcher in 1996, and you’ll understand the decade completely.
On one side of the room, you have the support vector machine, and it offered the things researchers had been starving for.
Start with the guarantee. Finding the maximum-margin boundary is what’s called a convex optimization problem. The word “convex” is doing real work here. Picture a smooth valley with a single lowest point. No matter where you start rolling a ball, it ends up at that same bottom. Convex problems are shaped like that valley: there’s exactly one best answer, and standard math will find it. Train an SVM on the same data twice and you get the identical result, provably the best one. No getting stuck, no luck involved.
Then there’s the theory, and this is where the story circles back to a name. Vladimir Vapnik, one of the two names on the method, had spent decades building the mathematics underneath it, a framework called statistical learning theory. It included a way to measure how complex a model is, and therefore how much it risks memorizing its training data instead of learning. (That measure carries his name: the Vapnik–Chervonenkis dimension, or VC dimension for short. Think of it as a number for how much rope a model has to hang itself with.) The margin connected straight to that theory. A wider street didn’t just feel safer; you could prove it generalized better. The method came with its own mathematical warranty.
And on top of all that, it barely needed babysitting. Just a couple of knobs, pick a kernel, done. Compare that to a neural network of the era, which needed you to choose the number of layers, the neurons in each, how to initialize the weights, the learning rate, when to stop: a dozen decisions that felt less like principle and more like folklore passed between grad students.
Now look at the neural network across the room, and be honest about what you see in 1996. It’s slow to train on the hardware you’ve got, hungry for far more data than anyone has, and riddled with finicky choices no theory can tell you how to make. Run the same net twice and you might get two different answers because it can get stuck partway and sit there in a mediocre solution. And it had one more problem: the deepest one. The problem this whole series has been walking toward.

The wall the neural networks kept hitting
Back in Episode 3, when we covered backpropagation (the algorithm that lets a network learn by passing its error backward, layer by layer, so each weight learns its share of the blame), I promised you there was a catch waiting. This is the catch. It’s called the vanishing gradient problem, and it’s the technical heart of why the neural nets lost — the reason the word “deep” was a fantasy for almost twenty years.
Remember how backprop works. You run an input forward, measure how wrong the final answer was, and send that error signal backward, handing each layer its portion of the fault so it knows how to adjust. The signal starts at the output and travels back toward the input, one layer at a time.
Here’s what nobody tells you until you feel it. At each layer the signal passes through on its way back, it gets multiplied by a n
